goals.description:
ALL students are provided with a rigorous and engaging educational program that develops their
communication, creativity, collaboration, and critical thinking skills. This will lead to students meeting
or exceeding grade-level standards in all core content areas and making progress in English
Language Development.

actions.title: Smaller Class Size
actions.description:
A ll students, including our ELs and Free and Reduced Price Lunch
students, are below standard in English Language Arts and Mathematics.
In ELA, all students were 21.2 points below standard and in Math they
were 50.2 points below standard in the Spring 2024 CAASPP. To ensure
all students receive targeted support, the district will maintain smaller class
sizes, when possible, especially in grades TK-3. This targeted support will
be delivered through focused small group instruction, differentiated
instruction, and progress monitored through various assessment
measures. Smaller class sizes will allow teachers to provide targeted
support and intervention in ELA and Mathematics and will lead to an
increase in academic achievement for all students. We expect to see an
increase in academic achievement in the CAASPP for ELA and Math for all
students in grades 3-8 and on the iReady assessment for students in K-2.

actions.title:
Seven period day in
Middle Schools
actions.description:
Each Middle School will continue to offer a seven-period day to ensure all
students, specifically our English Learners and students who qualify for
Free and Reduced Price Lunch (FRPL) are provided with support in
English Language Arts. At WMS, students receive two periods of ELA
instruction. Based on 2024 CAASPP data, at both of our middle schools,
our ELs are the lowest performing falling 76 points below standard in
English Language Arts and at WMS, they are 49.4 points below standard.
FRPL students are 18.4 points below standard (WMS) and 33.3 points
below standard (IMS). At WMS, a seven-period day allows class size to be
decreased facilitating teachers’ ability to provide focused support in ELA
and ensures there is sufficient time to cover the standards and provide
targeted in-class intervention. AT IMS, having a seven period day allows
students to have support in ELA and still have access to a broader course
of study to take elective classes. Access to a broad course of study
ensures students have opportunities to practice and generalize those skills
they learn in the core content ELA courses. Academic achievement for ELs
and FRPL students will be measured through the ELA CAASPP scores.

actions.title:
Multi-Tiered System
of Support (MTSS)
TOSA
actions.description:
According to our 2024 CAASPP data, all students scored 21.6 points
below standards in ELA and 50.2 points below standards below in
Mathematics. Students who qualify for Free and Reduced Price Lunch
(FRPL) demonstrate mastery at lower percentages than our overall student
group, 37.09% met or exceeded grade level standards in ELA and 27.24%
met or exceeded grade level standards in Mathematics. Due to the need to
support our student groups, the district will provide each school with a full-
time MTSS TOSA. This TOSA will support and coordinate the multi-tiered
system of support at each school site. This TOSA works alongside
teachers to ensure strong universal instruction (Tier 1) is provided to all
students and coordinates intervention support in ELA and Mathematics to
ensure students are placed in and receive targeted instruction (Tier 2)
during the instructional day. For the 2025-26 school year, the district will
implement a foundational reading supplemental programs to ensure all
students acquire the skills needed to become successful readers. The
MTSS TOSA coordinates with other school staff to ensure target students
receive intensive (Tier 3) support when needed. Additionally, the MTSS
TOSA uses data to monitor student progress and works with grade level
teams to problem solve and implement instructional strategies to support
student learning in all levels of instruction. The effectiveness of this action
is monitored through student yearly growth in the ELA and Math iReady
assessments and end of year CAASPP results.

actions.title:
Integration of
Educational
Technology
actions.description:
Since all students continue to score below standard in ELA (21.6 points
below) and Math (50.2 points below) and our FRPL students are 31.9
points below standard in ELA and 61.7 points below standard in Math
(2024 CAASPP Data), there is a need to integrate educational technology
to increase student learning. The use of technology is critical and will
develop all of our students' critical thinking, creativity, collaboration, and
enhance communication. These skills translate to student achievement in
English Language Arts, Mathematics, and Science. A full-time Educational
Technology Coach will promote and deepen student accessibility and use
of technology to deepen their learning. The Ed Tech Coach will target the
use of technology to leverage the teaching of Common Core ELA and
Math standards, Next Generation State Standards (NGSS), and students
acquiring essential skills for college and career readiness. The Coach will
provide professional learning opportunities through the coaching cycle,
professional training, lesson demonstrations, and modeling which will
result in increased student achievement in language arts, mathematics,
and science and will be measured through state assessments (CAASPP
and CAST).

actions.title:
Small Class Size in
Mathematics in
Middle School
actions.description:
Based on 2024 CAASPP data, at WMS, our FRPL students are 90.6 points
below standard in Mathematics. To lower the class size in math and
promote student learning and acceleration, the district will provide an
additional math teacher. The smaller class sizes in math will facilitate
teachers assessing student needs, provide small group instruction to
reteach content or provide targeted intervention. This will result in
improved achievement of our FRPL students in math as measured by the
CAASPP assessment and iReady yearly growth.

actions.title:
Supplementary
instructional
materials and
resources to
implement the
Common Core State
Standards (CCSS)
actions.description:
Our 2024 CAASPP data indicates our FRPL students demonstrate below
standard achievement in ELA and Mathematics, respectively 31.9 points
below standard and 61.7 points below standard and our ELs are 63.6 and
88.6 points below standard. There is a need to provide supplementary
materials that will support their specific needs. These additional resources
will be used during small group instruction or to provide additional practice
d uring whole group lessons. The district will provide supplementary CCSS
aligned curriculum resources in ELA and Math, NGSS, and History/Social
Science. Though this action specifically targets our FRPL students, all
students will have standards-based aligned supplementary instructional
materials and resources to support their learning in the core content areas.
Access to these instructional materials will ensure all students have the
access to the core curriculum and will make progress in formal state
assessments (CAASPP, CAST) and local assessments (iReady).

actions.title:
Dual Immersion
Program
actions.description:
The Dual Immersion Program at Ladera Palma (TK-6) and Washington
Middle School (7-8) provide students with the opportunity to develop
biliteracy skills and strengthen their academic achievement in Language
Arts, Mathematics, and Science. Student participation in these dual
immersion programs increases their cultural understanding and promotes
the development of college and career skills.

actions.title:
Local District
Assessments
actions.description:
Our 2024 CAASPP data indicates our FRPL students demonstrate below
standard achievement in ELA and Mathematics, respectively 31.9 points
below standard and 61.7 points below standard and our ELs are 63.6 and
88.6 points below standard. There is a need to provide a progress
monitoring assessment to measure student learning. To measure their
academic achievement growth, the district will invest in local assessment
measures to provide timely actionable data. Teachers will use this data to
focus and target their instruction to meet student needs and the address
instructional gaps of our FRPL and EL students. English Language Arts
and Mathematics progress will be measured for students in grades K-8.
The district will continue to utilize a Universal Screener K-8, and each
schools Professional Learning Teams (PLTs) will develop common
formative assessments to determine needs for Tier I and Tier II
interventions in ELA and math to increase student achievement. The
district will also implement appropriate assessments for the Dual
Immersion Program. This action will be measured through our FRPL and
EL student achievement results in the CAASPP for ELA and Math.

actions.title:
STEAM and VAPA
Integration
actions.description:
25.05% of our students met or exceeded the standards for Science as
measured by the 2024 California Science Test and 31.80% met or
exceeded the standards for Mathematics. Our ELs and FRPL, students
have a lower percentage of meeting standards in math (respectively 9.09%
and 27.24%) and in science (respectively 0% and 20.7%). Our students
require increased resources to learn and practice specific math and
science content and skills and should have opportunities to generalize,
apply, and deepen their learning through interdisciplinary experiences in
STEAM and VAPA. These opportunities for innovative and authentic
learning experiences will lead to increased achievement in Science,
Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Math (STEAM) and Visual and
Performing Arts (VAPA). To meet this need, we want to ensure our
teachers have resources they need to implement a high quality program.
We will provide staffing, academic coaches, content experts, training,
supplementary materials, and opportunities for professional learning to
support our student achievement in STEAM and enriched through VAPA.
These resources will allow our teachers to integrate STEAM and VAPA
into their lessons and will increase our student achievement in Math and
Science as measured by the CAASPP and CAST.

actions.title:
Student Physical
Wellness Linked to
Achievement
actions.description:
Based on our 2024 CAASPP data, our students score below standard in
ELA (21.6 points below) and Math (50.2 points below) and our FRPL
students achieve at lower levels. To ensure optimal learning, it is important
to support our FRPL student’s overall health and wellness. Meeting our
student’s physical needs correlates to increased achievement. The district
will provide staffing for a twice-weekly pull out physical education program
that will lead to enhanced wellness and fitness and provide classroom
teacher release to review data and plan student learning. Classroom
teachers have time during the school day to analyze data to provide
support during universal and targeted instruction to increase student
achievement in reading and math. During the PE release, the PE teacher:
maintains and monitors elementary physical education programs for
effectiveness; evaluates and supplement current nutrition education
through collaboration with local organizations; maintains and replenishes
physical education equipment and Fit Kid centers; works with local
agencies involved in the “Move More Eat Healthy” movement; provides
training to staff and parents on healthier choices. This action will lead to
increased achievement in ELA and Math as measured by the CAASPP.

actions.title:
After School Program
(ASES Funding)
actions.description:
The district will continue to provide an After School Education and Safety
(ASES) program for all students to extend learning opportunities in the
areas of academics, physical fitness, social emotional learning, and
enrichment on each site. Staff will be trained to support students
academically through tutoring, homework support, and problem-solving
skills that will lead to student achievement in reading and math. The After
School Education and Safety Program (ASES) implemented by the Boys
and Girls Club of La Habra is offered to all students, kindergarten through
eighth grade, and is located on each of LHCSD’s school sites. ASES offers
a safe, comprehensive, and academically focused program. We hire highly
qualified staff and train staff to assist students academically and socially.
The ASES staff will also support students by increasing academic support
in the program.

actions.title:
Early School
Readiness (UPK)
actions.description:
Our English Learners and FRPL families do not have the same level of
access to early childhood education learning experiences as more
resourced families. Providing access to UPK will ensure students have
increased opportunities to participate in a high quality TK program. Our
Universal Prekindergarten program targets enrolling EL and FRPL
students. The district supports this access by providing staffing (School
Readiness Coordinator and TOSA) to outreach, coordinate, and provide
training to TK teachers. Additional federal funding will be utilized to provide
increased access to early childhood education to our under-resourced
families and non-English speaking families. This funding will support
additional staff, which may include a Community Liaison, Nurse, and a
portion of the Early Learning TOSA. These staff members will provide
training and resources to families with children ages birth to five. Providing
access to quality early learning experiences will support the cognitive,
social-emotional, and physical development of children which will lead to
increased readiness skills of our TK and Kindergarten students. One way
this program provides support is through going free parent-child classes to
the La Habra Community. The School Readiness Nurse will provide health
referrals and information, screenings, and health-related resources for
families with children 0- 5 years. This will be measured through the iReady
progress of our Kindergarten students in ELA and Math.

actions.title: Technology Access
actions.description:
O ur FRPL students may not have access to the digital tools they require to
continue their learning at home or to complete their homework. Due to this
need, the district will support the implementation of the 1:1 iPad 24/7 take-
home program at all school sites. This will ensure our students have
access to the technology they need to learn at school and continue their
learning at home. This action supports providing an enriching and
engaging learning experience for all students; providing access to
instructional software and digital programs and platforms aligned to the
state standards; and provides parent education on the use of technology
and integration with California State Standards. This action is measured
through the CAASPP assessments in ELA and Mathematics.

actions.title:
Technology
Infrastructure
actions.description:
To ensure that all students, specifically our EL, FRPL, and Foster Youth
students have access to digital tools that support their learning, it is
important to have the infrastructure to ensure these tools are functioning at
optimal levels and are regularly updated. The district will provide iPads,
desktop computers, display systems, and other equipment and necessary
infrastructure to support an enriching and technology embedded
educational experience for all students. This action ensures we
consistently assess and address technology needs for classrooms, labs,
and multipurpose rooms; it provides the iPad refresh cycle; replaces and
upgrades existing equipment, as needed; and provides funds to purchase
any other related equipment and tools. Access to the technology tools will
increase student learning in English Language Arts and Mathematics,
demonstrating a growth in our student achievement as measured by the
CAASPP.

actions.title:
Technology
Infrastructure -
Staffing
actions.description:
To ensure that all students, specifically our EL, FRPL, and Foster Youth
students have access to the digital tools to support their learning, it is
important to have the infrastructure and staffing to ensure the tools are
functioning at optimal levels and are updated regularly. Information
Services Technicians (IST) will provide ongoing site-based technology
support to ensure the equipment and digital tools are functioning optimally
for student learning. This action provides first-level technical support, which
includes troubleshooting hardware and audio-visual issues and managing
our 1:1 iPad initiative for all sites. ISTs are responsible for planning,
organizing, keeping inventory, and providing technology support at all
sites. Access to the technology tools will increase student learning in
English Language Arts and Mathematics, demonstrating a growth in our
student achievement as measured by the CAASPP.

actions.title:
Instructional
Programs Support
actions.description:
Our current assessment data shows that our ELs, Foster Youth, and Free
and Reduced Price Lunch eligible students achieve at a lower level than all
students in the ELA and Math CAASPP. Due to this difference, the District
will ensure that our English Learners, Foster Youth, and Free and Reduced
Price Lunch eligible students have access to a high quality, rigorous
instructional program. To provide a high quality program and provide
assessments to monitor progress, compliance with state and federal
mandates is instrumental. District staffing will support the implementation
of a rigorous program and ensure we have ongoing formative and
summative assessments to monitor student achievement. District staffing
allows compliance with state and federal requirements for all academic
programs and supports the use of ongoing formative and summative
assessments for student learning. Through this support and monitoring,
our ELs, FY, and FRPL students will make academic progress in the ELA
and Math CAASPP.

actions.title:
English Learner
Achievement
actions.description:
On the 2024 CAASPP assessment, 6.92% of all ELs met or exceeded
standards for ELA and 9.09% met or exceeded standards for math. Within
the EL subgroup, 1.37% of our Long Term English Learners met or
exceeded the standards for ELA and 0% met or exceeded the standards
for math. These indicators demonstrate that our ELs are not at the
performing at the same level as the rest of the students. To monitor
progress, provide resources to instruct our ELs, and to provide
professional development training and support, the district will provide fund
one position, (Multilingual Program Specialist). This position will support
teachers’ implementation of research-based instructional practices in
English language development. Our goal is to close the gap in the early
grades to ensure our students do not become LTELs. Additionally, the ML
Specialist will provide targeted support and work with Imperial Middle
School and support their monitoring of ELs to increase their achievement
in ELA and Ladera Palma to ensure ELs make adequate progress. At
Ladera Palma, the ELPI is at 53% (increase of 14.9%), the Specialist will
work with the MTSS TOSA to support the cross linguistic transfer of skills
between Spanish Language Arts and English Language Arts. This will
increase English Learner progress toward English proficiency
demonstrating an increase in the percentage of students making progress
on the ELPAC and impact their increase their achievement in ELA and
Math, as measured by the CAASPP. For the 2025-26 school year, she will
also focus on K-2 Basic Reading Skills instruction to support ELs. For our
LTELs, the Program Specialist will monitor their progress (Ellevation), work
with the middle schools to create a program to support LTEL achievement
in ELA and Mathematics, and support teacher use of standardized
assessment resources (IABs) to assess and plan for LTEL student
learning.

actions.title:
Professional
Learning
Communities &
Teacher
Collaboration
actions.description:
In our most recent diagnostic assessment (Winter 2024), iReady, results
indicate that 31% of all students are two to three or more grade levels
below and 32% one grade level below in reading. Thus, 63% of our
students are not at grade level. In mathematics, 26% of all students are
two to three grade levels below and 44% or all students are one grade
level below; thus, 70% are below grade level in mathematics. Our FRPL
students and ELs achieve at lower levels and are not at grade level. Due to
the lower academic achievement of all students, specifically our FRPL and
ELs, it is necessary for our teachers to meet in Professional Learning
Communities. By incorporating the PLC process, teachers collaborate on
setting goals, focusing their instruction, planning for intervention, and using
data to assess student learning. Teachers also monitor the academic
progress and differentiate instruction, as needed, for our FRPL students
and English Learners. To enable this work, the district will continue to
schedule weekly early release enabling each site to have regular
structured PLC meetings. These meetings will focus on addressing the four
PLC questions: What do we want our students to learn?; How will we know
if each student has learned it?; How will we respond when some students
do not learn it?; and How can we extend and enrich the learning of
students who have demonstrated proficiency? This action will lead to an
overall increase in our iReady scores and we will see a growth between
the BOY and EOY results in both Reading and Math for all students.

actions.title: ELA Intervention
actions.description:
Current diagnostic data (Winter 2025) indicates 46% of all middle school
students are two to three grade levels below in reading. Our ELs and FRPL
students achieve at lower levels. To address this learning gap, the district
will provide a targeted reading intervention for middle school students. For
the 2025-26 school year, we will implement a new intervention program
and provide all students with scaffolds and support to access grade level
standards. Utilizing a collaborative model (general education teacher,
resource teacher, and instructional assistant), students will receive daily
targeted reading intervention which will lead to student growth in reading
skills. Research based intervention materials and targeted professional
learning for designated teachers will be provided to ensure effective
supports are implemented in the classroom. Through this action, we expect
to see a decrease in the percentage of students scoring below grade level
in reading, as measured by the iReady reading assessment.

actions.title:
Multi-Tiered System
of Support (MTSS)
actions.description:
Based on the 2024 CAASPP data, 19.5% of our McKinney-Vento students
m eet or exceed standards in ELA and 13.7% meets or exceeds standards
in math. They achieve at lower rates when compared to all students. The
percentage of English Learners and Free and Reduced Price Lunch
Students that are at grade level in reading and math is also lower than the
overall student group.
On the iReady reading assessment, 40% of our ELs and 23% of our FRPL
students in elementary school are two to three grade levels below in
reading and 90% of our ELs and 47% of our FRPL students are two to
three grade levels below in the middle schools. On the iReady math
assessment, % of our ELs and 19% of our FRPL students in elementary
school are two to three grade levels below in reading and 46% of our ELs
and 47% of our FRPL students are two to three grade levels below in the
middle schools. To support our ELs, FRPL including our homeless
students, the district will provide additional support staff to provide
universal and targeted instruction for ELs and FRPL students. This
additional staff will provide support through the Multi-Tiered System of
Support (MTSS) model in the areas of academic, behavioral, and social-
emotional needs with the goal to increase reading and math achievement.
Our homeless students will receive targeted support in mathematics.
Tailored systems inclusive of targeted academic instruction, Positive
Behavioral Interventions and Supports (PBIS), and Social Emotional
Learning (SEL) curriculum will be implemented at all sites in order to help
students succeed in school and improve academic performance in ELA
and Math. The goal is to provide high-quality instruction and intervention
matched to student needs. This action will increase our EL and FRPL
student achievement in reading and math measured by the EOY iReady
assessment.

actions.title:
English Learner
Achievement
actions.description:
Based on 2024 CAASPP data, 6.92% of all ELs met or exceeded
standards for ELA and 9.09% met or exceeded standards for math. Within
the EL subgroup, 1.37% of our Long Term English Learners met or
exceeded the standards for ELA and 0% met or exceeded the standards
for math. Specifically at Ladera Palma, 10% of ELs met standards in the
ELA CAASPP (increase of 2.42%) and 6.56% met standards in math
(decrease of 2.53%). To increase the academic achievement of this
student group, the district will provide additional staffing (certificated and
classified) for support. These staff will deliver targeted instruction and
intervention to our ELs. This support may include a combination of pull out
and push in services by teachers and/or aides. At Ladera Palma, a focus
on Thinking Maps, GLAD strategies, and trans-linguistic transfer will be the
focus for ELs. A continuous model of inquiry will be implemented to
ensure that students are making adequate progress. This model includes
the analysis of student data to ensure the targeted interventions are
meeting their needs. By providing this additional staff (assistants and
certificated support staff), we expect to see an increase in ELA and Math
CAASPP scores of our ELs.

actions.title: English Learners
actions.description:
Based on 2023-2024 ELPAC results, 19.97% of our English Learners
demonstrate well developed (Level 4) English Language skills and 36.56%
demonstrate moderately developed (Level 3) on the ELPAC. In
comparison, at Ladera Palma ES, 11.46% (increase of 1.46%) of all
students demonstrate well developed skills and 34.39% (increase of
3.18%) demonstrate moderately developed skills. The progress of our ELs
will be closely monitored by additional staff. Classified staff will ensure
student data is updated and accessible and a certificated staff member will
monitor assessment results and provide training and resources to teachers
to ensure they continually implement research based English Language
Acquisition strategies. At Ladera Palma, the certificated staff member will
collaborate with school staff to support the cross-linguistic transfer of skills
to reading and writing which will increase their ELPI. Parents will be
notified and kept informed of their student's progress. Additionally, the
certificated staff member will provide access to targeted PD in GLAD
strategies and Thinking Maps and work with all school sites to set
reclassification goals. Through this targeted monitoring and PD access, it
is expected that we will see a growth in the ELPI at Ladera Palma and
growth in our overall EL progress with an increase in our reclassification
rates.

actions.title:
Students with
Disabilities - English
Language Arts
actions.description:
In 3 of 7 elementary schools (Arbolita, Las Lomas, Sierra Vista) and both
middle schools (Imperial and Washington) our Students with Disabilities
are not making sufficient progress in English Language Arts. Their
achievement is at 3.13% (maintained at Las Lomas), 21.05% (increase of
12.23% at Arbolita), 13.64% (maintained at Imperial), 11.86% (decreased
by 2.22% at Washington), and 8.47% (decreased by 9.03% at Sierra
Vista). To increase their academic achievement, teachers will be provided
with additional resources and training in differentiating instruction to meet
student needs. Collaboration with the Special Education Department in
implementing high-leverage strategies will lead to better outcomes for our
SWDs. Based on this action, we expect to see an increase in the
percentage of students meeting or exceeding standards in ELA.

actions.title:
Students with
Disabilities -
Mathematics
actions.description:
In 4 of 7 elementary schools (El Cerrito, Las Lomas, Sierra Vista, and
Walnut) and one middle school (Imperial) our Students with Disabilities are
not making sufficient progress in Mathematics. Their achievement is at
6.07% (maintained at Imperial), 11.86% (maintained at Sierra Vista),
6.25% (Las decreased by 6.66%Lomas), 19.05% (increase of 5.33% at
Walnut), and 31.92% (decrease of 1.41% at El Cerrito). To increase their
academic achievement, teachers will be provided with a two day training in
CGI and have an opportunity to participate in a lesson study. At Imperial,
teachers will participate in a two lesson studies on building mathematical
discourse. Collaboration with the Special Education Department to support
implementation of CGI and other high impact strategies will lead to better
outcomes for our SWDs. Based on this action, we expect to see an
increase in the percentage of students meeting or exceeding standards in
Mathematics.

actions.title:
3.1 Safe and Clean
Learning
Environment
actions.description:
To provide schools that are safe and clean environments, the district will
ensure school and district facilities are maintained and they are safe
working and learning environments measured by the annual Facilities
Inspection Tool (FIT). This action provides staffing to maintain safe
facilities and prioritizes projects that promote positive learning
environments. This action will lead to an increase in the percentage of
students feeling safe in the annual LCAP survey. Students' perception of
safety will be measured through the LCAP survey.

actions.title: School Safety
actions.description:
According to 2025 LCAP Student Survey results, 15% of our students
indicate they feel unsafe at school. Additionally, 31% do not feel like they
belong at school, 27% do not feel unaccepted, and 31% do no have
positive relationships with staff. To increase school safety and promote
connectedness, the district provides a School Resource Officer (SRO). The
SRO supports the middle school campuses and is available to the
elementary schools sites, when needed. The SRO implements proactive
strategies to foster connection and belonging and provides mentoring to
students who are having difficulty feeling connected and positively
engaged at school. In addition to the SRO, the district provides part-time
staffing to promote overall wellness and safety. These part-time staff are
teacher leads who have the added responsibility of coordinating and
planning school site activities to promote safety, connection, belonging,
and wellness for all students. Examples of site activities may include
kindness events and anti-bullying campaigns. Through this action, we
expect to see an increase of in the percentage of students who feel safe at
school, accepted, have positive relationships with adults, and feel
connected at school as measured by the LCAP Student Survey.

actions.title: Transportation
actions.description:
Based on the 2024 CA Dashboard, our FRPL students demonstrate a
18.3% chronic absenteeism rate and declined by 0.2%, when compared to
our Spring 2023 attendance rates. These trends indicate a need to support
our students’ well being and increase their attendance rates. Home-to-
School Transportation will be provided to increase attendance rates and
will be provided to students living beyond three-fourths of a mile from their
school site who do not have means of transportation, targeting our
students who qualify for Free and Reduced Price Lunch. Through this
action, we expect to see a decrease in our FRPL chronic absenteeism
rate.

actions.title:
Monitor Attendance
Rates
actions.description:
Due to increased need to support our students’ well-being, attendance has
been impacted and our chronic absenteeism is currently at 16.7% (2024
Dashboard). According to the 2023 CA Dashboard, we had one student
group who has a very high chronic absenteeism rate, Filipino students
were at 21.4%. In the 2024 CA Dashboard, we decreased their chronic
absenteeism rate by 8.7% to 12.7%. We have four additional student
groups (LTELs at 24.4%; Foster Youth at 29.6%; McKinney-Vento at 40%;
and SWDs at 21.8%) that have very high chronic absenteeism rates. To
decrease our chronic absenteeism rate, additional staffing is provided to
monitor attendance. School office clerks closely monitor attendance, make
calls to parents as needed, and prepare and maintain appropriate forms to
track attendance. They work closely with the school administrator to
monitor students who are chronically absent or at risk of chronic
absenteeism. Schools then communicate with families and collaborate with
them through the MTSS to provide support and resources. For the 2025-26
school year, schools will monitor our identified student groups (FRPL,
Foster Youth, LTEL and McKinney-Vento) closely providing increased
proactive communication about the importance of school attendance and
immediately refer to the MTSS team when they are at-risk of chronic
absenteeism. In addition, monthly case management by an identified
school staff member will be an additional strategy for LTEL and McKinney-
Vento students. This action will lead to a decrease in chronic absenteeism
for all students.

actions.title: School Counselors
actions.description:
I n the 2025 LCAP Student Survey, 78% of students almost always or often
feel accepted at school and 78% of all students almost always or often feel
like they belong in school. 85% feel safe at school. In the 2024 California
Health Kids Survey (CHKS), a lower percentage of students indicated
feeling safe at school and 69% indicated they have caring relationships at
school. There is a need to provide social-emotional and mental health
support to increase connectedness and engagement. The district will
provide school-based counselors and counseling interns to support the
schools. Counselors will provide all students access to a developmentally
appropriate and comprehensive counseling program and social-emotional
learning activities. They will receive services based on the MTSS delivery
model. Universal support (Tier 1) may include consultation with the
teachers to implement general social-emotional and social skills activities
within the classroom. Through this level of support, counselors may
provide teachers with consultation, coaching, and training, community
circles, and SEL lessons. Targeted services (Tier 2) may include school
counseling lessons, group counseling sessions and intensive services (Tier
3) may include individual counseling and case management. In addition,
the school counselors will collaborate with other school staff, such as
school psychologists, to provide other necessary supports. Other services
may include indirect support such as referrals, consultation with teachers,
parents, and administrators, and collaboration with community partners.
This action will increase the percentage of students at each school who
report feelings of belonging and acceptance measured by the annual
LCAP survey.

actions.title: Suspension Rates
actions.description:
According to our Spring 2024 data, our overall suspension rate is 2.8%.
When compared to Spring 2023, we decreased our suspension rate by
0.5%. Our Foster Youth suspension rate currently at 5.4% and is
approximately double our overall rate. Additionally, our suspension rate for
two student groups increased, LTEL (15.6%) and Mc-Kinney-Vento (7.8%).
To decrease the suspension rate of our three student groups, our
counselors will work with teachers and administrators on implementing
positive behavior strategies, alternative to suspension strategies, and
monitor student behavior. Also, they will provide or support the
coordination of training in trauma informed and restorative practices.
Through this action, it is expected that we will see a reduction in the
suspension rate of all students, specifically LTEL, McKinney-Vento, and
Foster Youth students.

actions.title:
Chronic Absenteeism
-Other Student
Groups
actions.description:
According to the 2023 CA Dashboard, El Cerrito Elementary School's white
student group had a higher chronic absenteeism rate than the district
average, at 28.9% and at Las Lomas, Students with Disabilities (SWD) had
a 29.5% rate. Current data from the 2024 Dashboard indicates, El Cerrito
decreased the chronic absenteeism rate and it is currently at 0%. The Las
Lomas rate increased by 1.7% to 31.2%. The staffs at these schools work
closely with Student Services to implement strategies to increase the
attendance of these two student groups. They send targeted proactive
messaging to these families, work with families to provide necessary
resources, and implement attendance incentives for students to increase
their attendance. Focused efforts at Las Lomas will decrease the chronic
absenteeism rate during the 2025-26 school year.

actions.title: Communication
actions.description:
The 2025 LCAP surveys indicate 95% of all families feel welcome in their
child’s school and 97% feel comfortable communicating with their child’s
teacher and/or administrator. In the open responses, parents emphasized
the importance of clear communication between the school and parents,
particularly regarding events, programs, and student progress. These high
indicators demonstrate that the district and schools are communicating
with families and fostering a feeling of partnership and connection. To
continue with this effective communication and promote a culture of
respect, integrity, and inclusion, we will fund a position that sends out
timely and important communication to families. This includes supporting
district efforts with parent surveys, including but not limited to: LCAP
Family Survey, Student Engagement Survey, Title I Parent Survey, and EL
Parent Survey. Through this action we expect to continue with high
favorable ratings in communication, feeling welcome, and ability and ease
for families to communicate with school staff.

actions.title:
Bilingual Community
Liaisons
actions.description:
In the 2025 LCAP Survey, 88% of families strongly agreed or agreed that
school staff members make an effort to work with families to learn about
each student's strengths, cultures, and languages and 76% reported
knowing their Community Liaison (CL). 85% of families of EL students
reported they are notified of their student’s progress. There is strong
evidence that the schools’ staff are collaborating with families and
Community Liaisons are supporting these efforts between schools and
families. Open responses indicate a continued need to focus the
Community Liaisons' primary efforts to communicate and engage with
families. In the 2025-26 school year, the Wellness Program Specialist will
continue to provide targeted training to the CLs to increase their visibility
and communication efforts. The CLs will continue their focus on
home/school communication with our EL families, provide support to
families through regular parent education, make phone calls, and make
efforts to connect with families. Their efforts will support the schools with
promoting parent participation in parent literacy nights and other family
events. Based on these efforts, we expect that there will be an increase in
the percentage of families who know the Community Liaison and we will
increase the percentage of families who agree that the school staff makes
an effort to work with families in the LCAP Survey.

actions.title:
English Learner
Family Outreach
actions.description:
The district will provide parent learning opportunities to empower and
inspire the parents of English learners. Training and classes will focus on
Common Core State Standards, Literacy, College/Career Readiness,
Parent Participation, SEL, and STEAM Training. This will assist parents
with supporting their children's language development in English and their
overall achievement. This action will be provided through other funding
sources.

actions.title: Foster Youth
actions.description:
Our FY students face additional academic and social-emotional challenges
due to their unique life circumstances. FY students may experience trauma
and related social-emotional difficulties which may impact their ability to
focus, achieve, and follow behavior expectations. According to the 2024
CA Dashboard, our FY students have a higher suspension rate, 5.4%,
which is 2.6% higher than the overall suspension rate. As such, the district
will work with families of FY students and coordinate with the Orange
County Department of Family Services to provide support. The Foster
Youth Liaison will attend quarterly meetings at the Orange County
Department of Education (OCDE) to obtain updated information to support
Foster Youth and increase their behavioral and social-emotional outcomes.
This coordination will lead to a decrease in our suspension rates for Foster
Youth.

actions.title:
Students with
Disabilities
actions.description:
Based on the 2024 CAASPP Assessment, our students with disabilities are
significantly below grade level in English Language Arts and Mathematics,
respectively 85.9 points below in ELA and 113.1 below in Math. In addition,
in the 2025 LCAP Parent Engagement Survey, families of SWD reported
the following: 73% reported knowing where to find information about SPED
services; 64% reported parent training was provided; and 81% reported
receiving information and resources from their child’s school that help them
understand their child’s special needs and development. To ensure that we
provide timely information to our families of SWD, we will provide
opportunities to engage with district staff and collaborate on supporting
their students in the Special Education program. This may include
providing specific parent workshops, targeted communication efforts, and
participation in the SELPA Community Advisory Committee (CAC)
meetings. Better informed parents and increased knowledge of how to
support their students will support the overall education of our SWDs.
Through this related action, we expect to see an increase in the
percentage of parents strongly agreeing or agreeing that they receive
needed information, have opportunities to participate in targeted parent
workshops, and have access to the knowledge to better support their
students.

actions.title:
Targeted outreach to
families
actions.description:
Based on the 2024 CAASPP results, students who qualify for Free and
Reduced Price Lunch are 31.9 points below standard in ELA and 61.7
points below standard in mathematics. Our students who qualify for FRPL
may be under-resourced, not have access to specific support, may face
more challenging life circumstances. To support families of FRPL students,
the district will have focused family outreach efforts. This may include
specific family engagement learning and opportunities, workshops, and
family events. These opportunities will be based on the feedback provided
through the various district surveys. These opportunities can include
social-emotional learning, supporting your child with homework, and
implementing literacy and numeracy strategies at home. Additionally,
District staff will link students and families to needed resources such as
mental health support and referrals, referrals for basic needs, and other
supports, as needed. This action will provide the funding to provide
targeted training to support families in need and provide targeted parent
education classes. Through this related action, we expect to see an
increase in our student achievement in ELA and Math measured by the
CAASPP.

actions.title:
Professional
Learning
Opportunities
actions.description:
Our 2024 CAASPP data shows that all students continue to score below
standard in ELA (21.6 points below) and Math (50.2 points below). There
continues to be a need to support our teachers to increase our overall
student achievement and target the student groups that are scoring at the
lowest levels: LTEL students and SWDs score significantly below in ELA
and Math and are our lowest scoring students group at the district level
and our ELs, Homeless, and FRPL students achieve at lower levels in ELA
and Math when compared to our overall student group. Through targeted
professional learning and support of our teachers and staff, we expect their
individual and collective efficacy will lead to an increase in our overall
student achievement. LHCSD will provide professional learning
opportunities to support the implementation of the Common Core State
Standards in all grades. LHCSD staff (MTSS TOSAs, Technology
Integration Coach, and Program Specialists) and outside consultants will
provide direct instruction, modeling, coaching, and follow-up professional
learning and training. Training will include, but not be limited to, the
following research-based instructional strategies:
* Cognitively Guided Instruction (CGI)/Math
* Thinking Maps
* Project-Based Learning
* Guided Language Acquisition Design (GLAD)
* Early Literacy & Literacy for all
* English Language Development LHCSD staff (MTSS TOSAs and
Program Specialists) and outside consultants will provide direct
instruction, modeling, coaching, and follow-up training.
* Next Generation Science Standards (NGSS)
* Write From the Beginning & Beyond (WFTB&B)
* Professional Learning Communities (PLC)
Training will include, but not be limited to, the following research-based
behavioral and social-emotional strategies:
* Restorative Practices
* Trauma-Informed/Inclusive Practices
* Cultural Competency
* Positive Behavioral Intervention Support (PBIS)
Additional training provided to support LHCSD initiatives includes:
* Intervention Programs and effective use of iReady to progress
monitor student learning.
* Dual Immersion
* College & Career Readiness
* Multi-Tiered System of Support (MTSS)
* International Baccalaureate (IB) Primary Year and Middle Year
Programs
* Paraprofessionals will also receive training on many of the
strategies listed above.
* Visual and Performing Arts (VAPA)
* STEAM
To increase students' English proficiency, high-quality, professional
development will be provided to all teachers of English Learners on the
English Language Development (ELD) Standards. Our MTSS TOSAs will
support all teachers in the implementation of the ELD standards and
teachers will receive GLAD and Thinking Maps training and attend related
conferences. This action is funded through federal funds.
We expect to see academic growth for all students, ELs, FRPL, and SWDs
in the CAASPP in ELA and Math.

actions.title:
Teacher Induction
Program & Support
for New Teachers
actions.description:
Our 2024 CAASPP data shows that all students continue to score below
standard in ELA (21.6 points below) and Math (50.2 points below). There is
a need to support our teachers to increase our overall student
achievement. To ensure our new teachers have the necessary training and
support, we will provide support to assist them in fulfilling their professional
growth requirements for Clear Credentialing. Additionally, we will provide
new teachers with training in district-wide initiatives: Thinking Maps, CGI,
GLAD and Restorative Practices, etc. A credentialed Support Provider will
be assigned to all newly hired, preliminary credentialed teachers for two
years to assist them in fulfilling their professional growth requirements for
Clear credential status and to make a smooth transition to La Habra City
School District. This action will be funded through federal funds. We expect
to see an increase in student achievement as a result of this action.

actions.title:
Additional
Professional
Learning for College
and Career
Readiness
actions.description:
In our local indicators report, LHCSD strives to provide access to a broad
course of study to increase our students’ College and Career Readiness
skills. To develop our students’ skills and eventual eligibility for four-year
college/university requirements and equip them with the tools to access
21st-century careers, we will provide additional training opportunities to
teachers to ensure they provide students with opportunities to learn about
college and career paths and develop the necessary essential skills. Some
of this additional training supports the schools’ magnet foci and embeds
these college and career ready skills. This will include, but may not limited
to: Advancement via Individual Determination (AVID); No Excuses
University at Sierra Vista; Entrepreneurial Exploration at Las Lomas; Code
to the Future at El Cerrito; STEAM at Walnut; VAPA at Arbolita; Dual
Immersion at Ladera Palma and Washington Middle School; and
International Baccalaureate at Las Positas and Imperial Middle School.
Through this action, we will meet our self-assessment goal of providing
access to a broad course of study. In addition, this will lead to an increase
in student achievement in ELA, Math, and Science as measured by the
CAASPP and CAST.
